Reviews

Profiling Cell Signaling Networks at Single-cell Resolution[*](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#FN1)
================================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Xiao-Kang Lun‡§¶, Bernd Bodenmiller‡‖

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1074/mcp.R119.001790](https://doi.org/10.1074/mcp.R119.001790 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S1535947620350015&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Signaling networks process intra- and extracellular information to modulate the functions of a cell. Deregulation of signaling networks results in abnormal cellular physiological states and often drives diseases. Network responses to a stimulus or a drug treatment can be highly heterogeneous across cells in a tissue because of many sources of cellular genetic and non-genetic variance. Signaling network heterogeneity is the key to many [biological processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biological-phenomena-and-functions-concerning-the-entire-organism "Learn more about biological processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as [cell differentiation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cellular-differentiation "Learn more about cell differentiation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and drug resistance. Only recently, the emergence of multiplexed single-cell measurement technologies has made it possible to evaluate this heterogeneity. In this review, we categorize currently established single-cell signaling network profiling approaches by their methodology, coverage, and application, and we discuss the advantages and limitations of each type of technology. We also describe the available computational tools for network characterization using single-cell data and discuss potential confounding factors that need to be considered in single-cell signaling network analyses.

------------------

**Highlights**

*   •Signaling networks can be highly heterogeneous across cells in a tissue. 
*   •Various technologies allow analyzing signaling networks at single-cell resolution. 
*   •The advantages and limitations of each single-cell approach are summarized. 
*   •Confounding factors in single-cell signaling network analysis are discussed.

Cell-to-Cell Heterogeneity in the Signal Transduction Response
--------------------------------------------------------------

Signaling pathways mediate cell communication and coordinate cellular functions such as proliferation, differentiation, and energy metabolism ([1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib1), [2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib2), [3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib3), [4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib4)). They are often regulated by phosphorylation events mediated by kinases and phosphatases that result in the controlled activity of downstream effector molecules. Early research in signal transduction focused on delineating individual signaling pathways (or cassettes of signaling events) and understanding the enzyme-substrate relationships within these pathways. For example, after the discovery that [MAP kinases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mitogen-activated-protein-kinase "Learn more about MAP kinases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (mitogen-activated protein kinases) are serine/threonine kinases regulated by phosphotyrosine signaling, identification of both upstream kinases and downstream targets enabled the definition of “cascades” of specific phosphorylation events that respond to distinct cues for each MAP family member ([5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib5), [6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib6), [7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib7)).

Although this reductionist view of signaling events helped understand key principles of signal relay, it soon became apparent that signaling pathways are rarely independent within cells and living organisms, but are instead integrated. In general, crosstalk between two signaling pathways produces an output that differs from which would be triggered by only one of the pathways, and involves direct or indirect connections between the pathways ([8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib8), [9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib9)). For example, an [enzyme](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about enzyme from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in one pathway may directly phosphorylate and regulate a component of another pathway. Alternatively, indirect crosstalk can, for example, involve the transcriptional output of one pathway controlling the expression of components of another pathway ([8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib8)). Together with positive and negative feedback loops within pathways, these crosstalks can fine-tune or amplify signal in a context-dependent manner, resulting in a composite output ([10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib10)).

Although they remain essential to the understanding of signaling, [bulk](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/volume "Learn more about bulk from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") biochemical studies of pathways and networks, for example using global [phosphoproteomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/phosphoproteomics "Learn more about phosphoproteomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") profiling ([11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib11), [12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib12), [13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib13)), also do not account for cell-to-cell variability. At the single-cell level, network responses can be highly variable depending on cell type and on environmental conditions. These differences are because of many sources of [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and non-genetic heterogeneity in individual cells ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#fig1)).

![Image 9](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1535947620350015-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (321KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1535947620350015-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (321KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1535947620350015-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. **Signaling network heterogeneity in cell populations.**_A_, Mutated signaling [proteins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/phosphoprotein "Learn more about proteins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (e.g., kinases) may cause [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") heterogeneity in a population of cells and leads to differential signaling networks. _B_, Non-genetic signaling network heterogeneity may origin from [extrinsic factors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cyanocobalamin "Learn more about extrinsic factors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") including stimulus concentration, matrix stiffness, local crowdedness, oxygen and nutrient gradients, as well as the intrinsic noise. _C_, Signaling network heterogeneity results in phenotypical variances in a population of cells. [Bulk](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/volume "Learn more about Bulk from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") analysis averages these variances, resulting in misinterpretation of [cell signaling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction "Learn more about cell signaling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") network behaviors and cell phenotypes.

Genetic heterogeneity is often explained by mutations that affect the present and functionality of proteins and causes inherited phenotypical variability in a population of cells ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#fig1)_A_). Mutated signaling proteins may reshape signaling network structures and result in different response dynamics ([14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib14)). In cancer, [genomic instability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genome-instability "Learn more about genomic instability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") leads to the accumulation of mutations that results in discrete genetic abnormities that further the signaling response heterogeneity in cells from the same tumor ([15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib15)).

Non-genetic heterogeneity is known as differential phenotypes in cells sharing the identical genome. Causes of non-genetic heterogeneity include epigenetic regulation and so-called intrinsic and [extrinsic factors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cyanocobalamin "Learn more about extrinsic factors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Intrinsic heterogeneity denotes the inherent stochasticity of biomolecules present in a cell ([16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib16)) that may affect chemical processes involved in the [life cycles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/life-cycle "Learn more about life cycles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of an mRNA or a protein. Extrinsic heterogeneity is generated by factors that modulate the [transcriptome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transcriptome "Learn more about transcriptome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the [proteome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/proteome "Learn more about proteome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of cells in an ununiformed manner. Variables such as concentration of a stimulus (identities of neighboring cells), extracellular matrix stiffness ([17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib17)), local crowdedness and spatial constraints ([18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib18), [19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib19)), nutrient and oxygen gradients ([20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib20)), cell cycle and cell volume ([21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib21)) integratively provide heterogeneous signaling network responses in isogenic cells ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#fig1)_B_).

The signaling network heterogeneity resulting from these factors is crucial for [biological processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biological-phenomena-and-functions-concerning-the-entire-organism "Learn more about biological processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as [cell differentiation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cellular-differentiation "Learn more about cell differentiation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and tissue development ([22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib22)) and the maintenance of a functional bio-system ([23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib23)). During the progression of many diseases, including cancer, the signaling network heterogeneity also causes an increase in phenotypical complexity that may reduce the efficacy of therapeutic interventions ([24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib24)). For decades, these single-cell-level variances could not be systematically profiled because of technical limitations ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#fig1)_C_). Our understanding of signaling network behaviors in diseases is therefore often incomplete. The recent emergence of multiplexed single-cell measurement technologies has made it possible to profile signaling networks cell-by-cell. This allows to uncover the origin of genetic or non-genetic heterogeneity ([25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib25), [26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib26)), to analyze the variation of signaling networks affected by this heterogeneity ([27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib27), [28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib28)), and to evaluate downstream transcriptional and phenotypic effects induced by modulation of a signaling pathway or network ([29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib29), [30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib30)).

The Single-cell Era of Signaling Network Analysis
-------------------------------------------------

### Single-cell Analysis with High Multiplexity

Single-cell analysis has been performed since the invention of the microscope. Conventional microscopic methods are used to visualize cell structure, assess protein expression levels, and study cellular and subcellular spatial properties. These studies are facilitated by genetic or immunological fluorescent protein tagging methods ([31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib31)). Because of the limited multiplexing capacity of conventional microscopy, independent experiments using different batches of cells are generally required, resulting in the loss of relationships between assessed markers. This makes it challenging to study signaling mechanisms at the network level using conventional microscopy. The era of 'omics has made it possible to simultaneously measure [transcriptomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transcriptomics "Learn more about transcriptomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [proteomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/proteomics "Learn more about proteomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") information ([32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib32)). [Protein phosphorylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-phosphorylation "Learn more about Protein phosphorylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), one of the most critical post-translational modifications for signaling transduction, can be globally analyzed with [phosphoproteomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/phosphoproteomics "Learn more about phosphoproteomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") approaches ([33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib33)). The lack of sensitivity makes it challenging to apply these methods to single-cell measurements, however. Very recently, novel approaches that enable multiplexed antibody detection capacity ([34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib34), [35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib35), [36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib36), [37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib37), [38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib38), [39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib39)) and signal amplification ([40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib40), [41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib41), [42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib42)) have made it possible to explore cellular phosphorylation landscapes and signaling regulatory network structure cell-by-cell in heterogeneous samples. Here we summarize currently available approaches for signaling network analysis at single-cell resolution ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#fig2) and [Table I](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#tblI)).

![Image 10](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1535947620350015-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (227KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1535947620350015-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (227KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1535947620350015-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. Approaches to analyze [cell signaling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction "Learn more about cell signaling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") networks at single-cell resolution. Information on signaling network states in individual cells can be analyzed in cell suspension with [mass cytometry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mass-cytometry "Learn more about mass cytometry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which allows simultaneous measurement of about 50 markers such as phosphorylation levels of signaling [proteins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/phosphoprotein "Learn more about proteins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and markers of cell phenotype. Single-cell [RNA sequencing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rna-sequence "Learn more about RNA sequencing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technologies allow [transcriptomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transcriptomics "Learn more about transcriptomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") profiling that can be used to infer cell signaling states. Multiplexed cell signaling profiling can be performed _in situ_ with mass spectrometry-based imaging methods or with sequential immuno-based fluorescence imaging; these methods preserve spatial information. Live-cell imaging methods (e.g., kinase translocation reporters, FRET) can be used to monitor dynamic signaling behaviors in real time with single-cell resolution, although with lower multiplexing capability.

Table I. Comparison of single-cell approaches for signaling network analysis

| Technique | Multiplicity | Through put | Cost | Sample type | Target of measurement | Spatial resolution | Sensitivity |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Flow cytometry | Up to 30 | Very high | Low | Single cells stained with fluorophore-conjugated antibodies | Proteins and protein modifications. High number of additional assays available. | N/A | High |
| Mass cytometry | Up to 50 | High | Low | Single cells stained with metal isotope-conjugated antibodies | Proteins, protein modifications and transcripts | N/A | High |
| Single-cell immuno-sequencing (CITE-seq and REAP-seq, etc.) | Unlimited | Medium | High | Single cells stained with DNA oligonucleotide-labeled antibodies | Proteins and protein modifications | N/A | Medium |
| Lab-on-chip and microfluidics (SCBC and scWesterns) | 10 | Medium | Low | Single-cell lysis | Proteins and protein modifications | N/A | High |
| Single-cell proteomics | Unlimited | Very low | High | Single-cell lysis | Proteins and protein modifications | N/A | Low |
| Single-cell RNA-seq | Unlimited | Medium | High | Single-cell lysis | mRNA | N/A | Medium |
| Multiplexed imaging based on sequential antibody staining (MELC, MxIF, CycIF, 4i, etc.) | Up to 90 | Medium | Low | Fixed cell or tissue slides | Proteins and protein modifications | High | High |
| Multiplexed imaging based on sequential antibody detection (immune-SABER and CODEX, etc.) | 30 | High | Low | Fixed cell or tissue slides | Proteins and protein modifications | High | High |
| Imaging mass cytometry (IMC) | Up to 50 | Medium | Medium | Fixed cell or tissue slides | Proteins and protein modifications | Medium | Medium |
| Multiplexed ion beam imaging (MIBI) | Up to 50 | Medium | High | Fixed cell or tissue slides | Proteins and protein modifications | High | Medium |
| MALDI-based imaging | Unlimited | Medium | High | Fixed tissue slides | Lipids and metabolites | Low | Low |
| _In situ_ sequencing (FISSEQ) | Unlimited | Low | High | Fixed cell or tissue slides | mRNA | High | Low |
| Fluorescence _in situ_ hybridization (MERFISH and seqFISH, etc.) | 100s | Low | Low | Fixed cell or tissue slides | Genomic DNA and mRNA | High | High |
| Kinase translocation reporter | 3 | Medium | Low | Live cells | Kinases | High | High |
| FRET | Up to 6 | Medium | Low | Live cells | Kinases or interactive proteins | High | High |

### Non-spatial Single-Cell Analysis Based on Immunological Approaches

#### Flow Cytometry

Flow [cytometry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cytometry "Learn more about cytometry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") uses fluorophore-labeled antibodies to detect and quantify protein abundance in individual cells. It has been used to monitor relationships between multiple phosphorylation sites and correlations between phosphorylation states, functional readouts, and lineage-specific markers in complex populations of cells ([43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib43)). With the capability to simultaneously measure ∼10 (up to 30 in more advanced setups) phosphoproteins and [phospholipids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/phospholipid "Learn more about phospholipids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), flow cytometry-based single-cell analysis has recently been combined with inhibitor perturbation assays enabling the inference of signaling circuits and the reconstruction of signaling networks ([44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib44)). The development of fluorescent cell barcoding has greatly increased the throughput of flow cytometry-based [intracellular signaling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/intracellular-signaling "Learn more about intracellular signaling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") analysis. It is now routinely implemented as a screening tool to quantify cellular responses to kinase inhibitors in individual cell types in heterogeneous populations ([45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib45), [46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib46)). However, because of the overlap of the fluorescent spectra of the fluorescent dyes used to label antibodies, the number of markers that can be analyzed simultaneously by flow cytometry remains limited, and signaling networks can only be sparsely or partially interrogated using this technique. Nevertheless, with the advantages of throughput and accessibility, flow cytometry is one of the most used methods for single-cell signaling assessments in research and diagnosis ([47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib47), [48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1535947620350015#bib48)).

enriched_points.expanded_content: The study primarily focuses on specific signaling pathways, limiting insights into broader cellular signaling networks and their interactions, potentially overlooking crucial regulatory mechanisms.
Data used in this study may suffer from heterogeneity and lack of comprehensive annotation, impacting the overall reliability and applicability of the findings in real biological contexts.
Future studies should incorporate advanced single-cell techniques to explore signaling heterogeneity, enhance data integration from multiple sources, and develop more accurate predictive models.
